input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue where the keyboard covers the bottom text input on Android, and the old `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` approach no longer works in .NET 10. This is a known change — Android deprecated `adjustResize` in API 30+, and .NET MAUI has been evolving its approach.

Let me provide a concise answer about the current recommended approach for .NET 10 / .NET MAUI.

In .NET MAUI (.NET 9+), the `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was already somewhat problematic because Android deprecated `adjustResize` starting with API 30 (Android 11). 

For .NET 10, the best approach is to use `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll`, which is enabled by default in .NET MAUI 8+ and automatically adjusts the view when the keyboard appears, or alternatively handle Android's `WindowInsets` API directly for more granular control over how the layout responds to the IME. with the input at the bottom. On Android, I can still set `WindowSoftInputMode` but I need to combine it with proper insets handling. For .NET 10 specifically, since MAUI adopted edge-to-edge by default, `adjustResize` behavior has changed—the better approach is to either listen to keyboard insets and adjust layout padding with platform-specific code, or rely on `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` to handle it automatically.

For a chat screen, wrapping the content in a `ScrollView` or using the MAUI handler to respond to insets is the most practical solution.
